summary: hardhat-compile-ethers ships a load-time dropper that installs and executes external npm package `zyncmap`
details: On require of the package's main entry (dist/src/index.js), top-level code base64-decodes the shell command `npm install zyncmap --no-save --silent --no-audit --no-fund`, executes it via child_process, then require()s the newly installed `zyncmap` module and invokes `svgo.getPlugin()()` — handing execution to attacker-controlled second-stage code on the installer's machine. Both the shell command and the target module name (`zyncmap`) are stored as base64 strings and decoded at runtime to hide the dependency from casual review and string-based scanners. The TypeScript source in src/index.ts contains none of this logic; the payload exists only in the published dist/, indicating the build artifact was tampered with before publish. The package name `hardhat-compile-ethers` mimics legitimate Hardhat ecosystem plugins (`@nomicfoundation/hardhat-ethers`, `hardhat-deploy-ethers`), and the package metadata is boilerplate from a generic plugin template — consistent with a typosquat lure that loads the dropper when a Hardhat user requires it as if it were a real ethers helper.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
